sentences.text: Yeah.
Four pictures.
Yeah.
I was born on October 11, 1914, in the town of Hamborn, Duisburg, in Germany.
Beg your pardon?
My full name is John Damski.
When-- when I was four years old, it was 1919.
My parents moved back to Poland.
And I was living first in a little town, Koronowo, then in a town Solec-Kujawski, what was in the northern part of Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's where I went to school, to the grammar school.
And my father was a police officer.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after finishing the grammar school, I went to the town Bydgoszcz, it was a larger town, where I went to a technical college.
And there I became an electrical technician.
My mother was a housewife, and she died in 1942.
I was brought up as a Catholic.
My whole family was Catholic.
Yes, we were.
We were not very-- very religious, but we practiced the religion, just like any other Catholic family.
My-- my father was an orphan when he was 11 years old, so I didn't know the parents of my father.
However, my mother's parents, they were alive.
My mother's maiden name was Stanislava Nowak.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my grandfather was Adam Novak, grandmother was Gryszinska [?
Delphinia. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They did not live in the same city that we did.
Father had a-- grandfather had a business-- shoe business.
And I had-- my mother had three brothers.
Two of them were mayors in small cities in Poland.
And at-- at one time, I had three relatives that were mayors in little towns.
So it was a family of-- I can say-- bureaucrats.
And I was very active from my childhood in sports.
I was in track and field, and I was a soccer player.
Excuse me.
As a matter of fact, in 1939--

In 1939, I was asked by the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] did I say already-- by the president of the soccer ball association if I would be available to attend a camp, what they call an Olympic [? cutter. ?] I would eventually be playing in the Olympics in 1939 in Helsinki.
However, the war broke out, and the Olympic dreams just went with the war.
Now, when the war broke out, I was in the Army.
I had two brothers and two sisters.
One brother was-- the oldest was Zigmund Damski, then was my sister [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sophia Damski, then a brother, Vadislav Damski, and the youngest sister, Janina.
She is still alive and lives in Warsaw.
Right.
I was the second from the top.
The second oldest.
One brother was older, yes.
And one was younger.
Well, we lived-- most of my-- from-- from 10 years-- when I was 10 years old, we lived in a town Solec-Kujawski, which was a small town, about four and a half town population.
That's where my father was the-- the-- the commandant of the police station there.
And that-- we had two or three Jewish families in our city.
And I was going to school with-- with one of the Jews.
And we were very good friends, and so was my sister.
As a matter of fact, he sneaked out to our house on Sunday to eat something that was not kosher.
[CHUCKLES] But-- and the life in that little town was, as far as relations, Jewish-Polish relations, was-- was very, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can say, very tolerant.
They belonged to the same clubs that we did.
We were playing on the same team, soccer.
And the older brother of that family was a-- a bicyclist, on the bicycling team.
So there was actually no tension whatsoever.
And beside that, my father was very-- a very tolerant man.
A good man.
And there was a time when the last family moved from our house, and my brother came home, and I said, father, did you know that the Dalmans are moving out?
So my father said, that's very bad.
But we still would stay because wherever the Jews are, something's happening.
That was the reaction of my father.
Something's happening meaning--
Something's happening, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how can you say-- the Jews are more active, in-- in-- like-- like all over the world.
And when sometimes anybody would be anti-Semitic in the presence of my grandmother, she always reminded them that Christ was a Jew, and that always closed the conversation.
So I was brought up, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not especially to-- to be Philo-Semite, but we just accepted the Jews like our own, and that was-- that was fine, I think.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So after the war, I-- can I start when I stopped-- when the war broke out?
When the war broke out, I was in the army.
I was in a battalion of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the seashore-- defend, kind of a battalion Obrona Wybrzeza, a battalion to protect the seashore.
And the war didn't-- didn't last very long, only a month in Poland, and we were captured.
But I did not go to the camp because I ran away at night and changed my clothes into civilian.
And I was working for a short time in the same town.
It was the town Gdynia, at the seaport.
I was working for the seaport administration at this time, when the war broke out.
And one day, I saw in the street one of-- of my acquaintances, and he said to me, Heil Hitler.
So I just told him something nasty, and he said, no, no.
He said, I'm very serious.
And his name was Nogalski, a Polish name, but he was a German.
And he asked me if I-- what am I doing.
I said, I'm not doing anything.
I don't have any job.
So he asked me to work for the administration of the water and the water department.
So I was working for-- there for about two months, and one day, the director told me when I am going to declare myself.
And I said, what are you talking about?
He said, well, aren't you going to declare yourself that you are a German?
Since I was born in Germany.
And I said no, I'm a Pole-- I'm Polish.
So he practically kicked me out of his office.
And then I went-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] part of the town of Gdynia, which is next to Gdansk, population was-- was-- they call it [NON-ENGLISH], which means we had to leave the town.
I think it was only a few hours' time, and we could take very little with us, and we had to move out of the town, to the southern-- southern part of Poland.
All Poles had to move out of the town?
Not all of them.
They had thrown some in the street in a group, a group.
So I-- I went to the-- there was a Generalgouvernement.
Part of the western and southwestern Poland was annexed to the Grossdeutsches, what they say, Greater Germany.
But there was a Generalgouvernement, which is in Warsaw and Cracow that belonged to the Generalgouvernement.
And we moved to that part-- only Poles moved to that part of Poland.
However, I decided to go to France.
I was trying to join the Polish army in France.
But I was caught not far from the Polish-Hungarian boundary.
And I was thrown in a jail that was administered by the Gestapo, in the town of Sanok.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The jail had mostly political prisoners.
As-- as an example, I will-- I'll give you an example.
We were 345 prisoners.
In one night, June 13-14, they shot 115.
I was arrested on December 9th-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] December 7th-- pardon me-- 1939.
So I was there, and after they shot those 115, they transported the rest of the people to Auschwitz.
However, I was not in the group that was transported.
And I was alone in a cell.
If you want me to, I could describe the-- the life in the cell.
The cell was exactly 11 paces.
I know, because I was the oldest one.
I was privileged to walk in the middle.
And we were 45 prisoners in that one cell.
There was not enough room to lay on our backs.
We could sleep only on our sides.
And as I said, after they evacuated all of them to Auschwitz, I was-- in the-- in the cell.
And one day-- I was interrogated before that, many times.
Any time you were interrogated, you were beaten.
I was not beaten very badly because I spoke German, and that always helped.
You could say something to the-- to the fellow that beat you.

And-- and now-- and-- one day, as I said, an SS man or Gestapo man came to the cell and asked me-- could I say it in the Germany language? 'Was machen sie' here, which means, what are you doing over here?
And I said, well, I just was arrested, and that's it.
So he asked me where I was arrested, and I did not tell him where I was arrested.
I told him a lie, that I was arrested on the street.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that ended the conversation.
And half an hour later, they came to me and took me to interrogation, to the office of the Gestapo.
So in that office, the fellow that started the interrogation told me [GERMAN].
He asked me my name and so on.
He said, [SPEAKING GERMAN] which means, I don't have any records of you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from that moment on, I started to lie.
I told him that I was brought up as a German, which was no true, because we never spoke German in Poland, even in Germany.
And I told him that my mother was German, though she had a Polish name, that she was German.
That my father was in the German army, which was true, because during WWI, my father was in the German army, like everybody from the occupied territories.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he interrogated me for two hours.
I had to give all kinds of arguments that I was brought up as a German.
I told him in the first place, he'd never find a Pole that speaks Polish like I do.
And I told him muttersprache-- like told him the mother's language, just to tickle a little bit his German nationalism.
And when I told him that, he said, you are a German.
And he said, I will release you, but I want you to disappear in 24 hours.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he gave me a piece of paper, and they took me back to the cell, and about an hour later, somebody came, opened the door, and said, you're going out.
I was there nine months.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So when I went out, two girls came to me outside of the prison.
They took me under their arm, and took me to their home, where I could get-- excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I got a toothbrush and soap so I could wash myself.
No, I didn't know them.
They were girls from the underground.
What makes me think-- there are two things that could happen.
Why there were no-- no record of me.
Perhaps the underground somehow got my records out.
That's one possibility.
The other possibility is that-- that my aunt sent a German officer whom she knew from the town of [NON-ENGLISH], which is in the northern part of Poland.
He-- he was going to Sanok to see if he can get my release.
He came home and-- came home and told my aunt that there was no way that I could be released.
I was probably was designed to be shot like the other guys, however something happened, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I got out.
When I got out, I went to the town of Lublin, where my brother lived-- older brother.
And from that town, we went to a small town, not a small--
Well, I didn't have any money.
I was-- when I was waiting-- I got out of the cell, and I stood with my hands behind, waiting for somebody to lead me out.
And there was a woman, a warden, and she put something in my hand.
I didn't look, but I knew something was in my hand.
When I got out there was, like, $5.
So my dream was to get something to eat because we were fed very badly.
I was only 97 pounds when I got out, and that $5 took- that's what I had to pay for the dinner.
And the next day, I didn't have any money for the railroad, so I went from behind-- I didn't enter the railroad station from the front.
I went from behind and went into the-- the-- the-- wagon.
And so I went to the town of Cracow.
There were two places where we had to go out.
And when I came to the town Cracow, I was still without any money.
It was a time when Hitler called all the [SPEAKING GERMAN], [GERMAN], in German, back to Germany.
And there was, they call it [GERMAN], bessarabiandeutsche, which means a meeting place for bessarabiandeutsche, so I joined the group.
And I went with them.
And for two days, I got something to eat, and I had some place to sleep.
And then, the same way, without money, I went to the town of Lublin.
But in the-- in the middle of the-- of the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] of the travel, the fellow who was taking tickets came to me, and I told him I got out of prison, I didn't have any money.
So he locked me up.
He locked me up, didn't say anything.
But when we came about-- a few miles from Lublin, he opened the door.
So he was, evidently, a fine Polish fellow that helped me.
Now, at Lublin with my brother, we went to a smaller town, where we thought it would be easier to survive the war.
It was the town of Zamosc.
That's where my present life-- wife lived with her family.
And there I was working for a company called Bauleitung der Luftwaffe, which was a company that built airfields.
And there was-- I was hired by the chief engineer, and he told me to organize so-and-so many electricians.
Since I couldn't find, in that town, any electricians to my standard, I got the idea that I'd go to a Jewish-- there was a small Jewish labor camp.
I got the idea, I went up there-- if there are any Jews.
The-- the commander of that camp got them all together and said all the electricians-- is anybody an electrician?
Yeah, yeah.
So about 12 guys stepped out.
I found out there was only one electrician.
They all had nothing to do with the electrical business, but-- but they wanted out.
And I was able to accommodate them in the school where we had the magazine-- there was a school that was a place where the German craftsman lived, and I managed to get one classroom.
I got only 12 guys, I believe, in that classroom.
And I was able to get them some-- some blankets, and they stayed there.
They worked in the-- I had a magazine up there with a lot of electrical equipment, because we were building six airfields around that-- that town of Zamosc.
For the Germans, before the war with Russia.
They were preparing for the war with Russia.
One day, there was an evening, one of the Germans came to me and stopped, hollered and cursed me, how could I do it that he has to sleep under the same roof with the Jews.
So there was the-- I insulted his dignity by letting the Jews sleep under the same roof.
They had to go out.
But they found [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] quarters among the Jewish families in the city.
And so they were working for me, and I became quite-- how can I say it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] involved in their everyday life.
As a matter of fact, despite that there was a war, there was still a wedding going on.
I was invited to the wedding.
The first time in my life I saw a Jewish wedding.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had more Jews working for me, gradually.

Most of them were working for-- in the-- in the magazine, as a couple of-- of-- of-- what do you call it-- [GERMAN], or administrators of-- or helpers in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] materials department.
I only had two engineers working for me.
One was-- name was Bromberg, the other one had a Polish name-- oh, Prashka.
And they both died.
They both died when they started to exterminate.
Maybe I shouldn't use that language-- when they started to transport Jews to the death camps.
I was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as a matter of fact, some of them were my very good friends.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At this time, I-- I met many Jewish-- Jewish girls.
I was young.
Beg your pardon?
That was from 1940.
In 1940, when I got out-- out of prison, I was living in the town of Zamosc.
And the first time that I was-- was arrested was when I was speaking to a Jewish girl.
She was a young girl, younger than I was, and I walked-- walked her home.
And the curfew hour for the Poles was 8 o'clock.
For the Jews, it was 7 o'clock.
I walked her home, and she was on the other side of the fence.
I was on this of the fence.
I had one hour-- more hour to speak with her.
And then the gendarmerie came.
Somebody had tipped them off.
I knew, I knew who had tipped them off.
And they beat me up.
They put me in jail.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they were good enough to call my superior at the Bauleitung.
And he came.
He told them how-- how essential I am to him.
And then he over-- I'll never forget it.
His name was Endeline, Walter Endeline.
He was a famous engineer.
He said, I don't know of any law that forbids the Poles to speak to Jews, he told them at the police station, and I got out.
That was the first time I got arrested.
There were-- across the street, I saw some women.
Because, when they beat me up, you know, right there on the spot, one of them hollered that I got hit, and my hat fell off.
You know, they were laughing, they thought it was funny that I got beaten up.
They probably didn't like me to speak to the Jewish girl.
They don't like [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at the same time, I met my present wife's sister-- younger sister.
And she introduced me to her family.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And from then on, I was quite-- not often, but every few days, I was in their home, talking with-- with their father.
And you know, they had-- they had their problems, and we talked about it.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day, I had to send Jews out to work, but they were always late.
Instead of being there at 8 or 9 o'clock, they were there at 1 o'clock, 12 o'clock, so they arrested me for sabotage.
I was arrested by-- by an officer or two officers from the army-- from air forces, pardon me.
And a few soldiers [INAUDIBLE] were parading me through the village where the airfield was built.
I was, at that time, at the airfield.
And they put me in-- in a jail.
I was there for two or three days, but I got out because my-- my superiors needed me.
They wanted me to become a German.
They gave me all kinds of propositions and promises.
They would send me to-- to-- College of the Luftwaffe engineers, that means engineers for the-- for the air forces.
And I told that fellow, who was a very, very fine guy-- Yenkner was his name.
The guy-- as a matter of fact, that was speaking to Hitler once in while.
And he told me that I-- made me all the promises of what they were going to do for me.
I have a big opportunity.
And I told him, unfortunately, I said, but I have to declare myself as a German.
Now, there again, the situation-- I told him that I cannot do it because I'm Polish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And one moment, he still tried to persuade me.
I asked him, what would you do in my place?
Now, that fellow, he was a rank of-- of-- of-- of [GERMAN] was his title.
And he told me [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means, I would whistle on it.
And he said, from now on, nobody's going to bother you anymore.
He said, you can work over here.
Nobody's going to bother you or force you to become a German.
So there was-- out of the thousands of Germans that I knew, there were a few of them that had a little bit humanity in them.
